大学南校は、明治5年[[8月3日 (旧暦)|8月3日]]（[[1872年]][[9月5日]]）の[[学制]]頒布により「'''第一大学区第一番中学校'''」（学制における中学校）へと改編されたが、この頃には外国語による「普通科」の課程を修了する学生が出てくるようになった。このため、[[1873年]]（明治6年）[[4月10日]]には「専門学校」に転換され、再び「'''（第一大学区）開成学校'''」と称した。この際、教授言語は原則として英語に統一されることとなり、8月、従来の「語学課程」（普通科）に加えて新設された「専門学課程」（専門科）では法学・化学・工学・鉱山学・諸芸学の五科が設置されたが、法学・化学・工学が英語で教授されたのに対し、鉱山学はドイツ語、諸芸学はフランス語で授業が行われ、残留していた独仏語専修の学生に対する移行措置とし、この2学科については学生の卒業にともなって順次廃止した。同年11月4日には[[学制]]二編追加に基づき、語学課程が[[東京外国語学校 (旧制)|東京外国語学校（旧外語）]]（[[東京外国語大学]]などの前身）として分離独立した。同年12月、初代校長に[[畠山義成]]が就任した{{Cite book |和書 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%95%A0%E5%B1%B1%E7%BE%A9%E6%88%90-1101247 |title=畠山義成 |publisher=デジタル版 日本人名大辞典+Plus }}。残る専門学課程は、翌[[1874年]]5月「'''東京開成学校'''」への改称により、法学・化学・工学三科よりなる修業年限3年ないし4年の「本科」に再編され、さらに修業年限3年の「予科」が設けられた。またこの年、工業関係の実務者を簡易速成するための「製作学教場」が設けられた（1877年まで）。1875年、本科生・予科生から11名が選抜され、文部省派遣による第一回の海外留学生となった。 [[1877年]]2月1日に東京開成学校綜理（校長）に就任した[[加藤弘之]]は開成学校を「開成大学校」に昇格させるべきとした意見書を文部省に提出、これにより[[4月12日]]には、東京開成学校本科は、[[東京医学校]]と統合し、[[法学|法]]・[[理学部|理]]・[[文学部|文]]・[[医学部|医]]4[[学部]]よりなる'''[[東京大学 (1877-1886)|（旧）東京大学]]'''に改編された。また予科は、同じく官立[[外国語学校 (明治初期)#東京英語学校|東京英語学校]]との統合により[[東京大学 (1877-1886)#大学予備門|東京大学予備門]]（[[高等中学校|第一高等中学校]]、のちの[[第一高等学校 (旧制) |一高]]の前身）となった。しかし[[1881年]]の組織改革までの東京大学は、事実上旧東京開成学校と旧東京医学校の連合体に過ぎず、開成学校の後身である法理文三学部は独自の「綜理」（現在の学長に相当し4月13日[[加藤弘之]]が就任した）を戴くなど独立性が強かった（この後の経緯については[[東京大学 (1877-1886)|当該項目]]へ）。 == 主な出身者 ==
*[[菊池武夫 (法律家) |菊池武夫]] *[[穂積陳重]] *[[鳩山和夫]] *[[三宅雪嶺]] *[[嘉納治五郎]] *[[山口半六]] *[[松村任三]] *[[山崎為徳]] *[[新井領一郎]] *[[杉浦重剛]] *[[増島六一郎]] *[[中村弥六]] *[[田中舘愛橘]] *[[井上毅]] *[[吉松茂太郎]]
*[[野村龍太郎]] *[[宮崎道三郎]] *[[福島安正]] *[[末岡精一]] *[[田中稲城]] *[[大井憲太郎]] *[[高橋卯之助]] *[[平田東助]] *[[清水郁太郎]] *[[村岡範為馳]] *[[佐藤昌介]] *[[伊沢修二]] *[[久原躬弦]] *[[田原良純]] *[[中江兆民]]
*[[島田三郎]] *[[岸本辰雄]] *[[大槻文彦]] *[[高橋是清]] *[[高橋順太郎]] *[[濱尾新]] *[[小村壽太郎]] *[[隈本有尚]] *[[牧野伸顕]]
*[[小林有也]] *[[永井久一郎]] *[[高橋由一]] *[[古市公威]] *[[九鬼隆一]] *夏目大助（[[夏目漱石]]の兄） *[[宮城浩蔵]] *[[小菅智淵]] *[[志賀泰山]]
{{clear|left}} == 教職員 == [[ファイル:Birthplace of Tokyo University.jpg|thumb|right|200px|「東京大学発祥の地」の碑 / かつての開成学校の所在地（[[神田錦町]]）に建立されている]] *[[グイド・フルベッキ]]（蘭） *[[マリオン・スコット]]（米） *[[ゴットフリード・ワグネル]]（独） *中浜万次郎（[[ジョン万次郎]]） *[[濱尾新]] *[[ウィリアム・グリフィス]]（米） *[[小泉信吉]] *[[加藤弘之]] *[[山川健次郎]] *[[外山正一]] *[[エドワード・サイル]]（英） == 校地の変遷と継承 == 旧[[開成所]]から引き継いだ[[神田 (千代田区)|神田]][[一ツ橋]]（現在の[[神田錦町]]）の校地（[[湯島]]に所在していた大学本校の南に位置していたため、これが「大学'''南'''校」の由来となった）に所在しており、[[東京大学 (1877-1886)|東京大学]]発足後もしばらくは法理文三学部の校地として使用されていたが、[[1885年]]までに[[東京大学本郷地区キャンパス|本郷校地]]へ順次移転した。現在、神田錦町の跡地には「東京大学発祥の地」の碑が建てられている。 == 関連文献 == ;単行書 * [[寺崎昌男]] 『東京大学の歴史：大学制度の先駆け』 [[講談社学術文庫]]、[[2007年]] ISBN 9784061597990 * [[天野郁夫]] 『大学の誕生（上）：帝国大学の時代』 [[中公新書]]、[[2009年]] ISBN 9784121020048 ;事典項目 * 麻生誠 「高等教育〈学制以前〉」 『日本近代教育史事典』 [[平凡社]]、[[1971年]] * 小林哲也 「高等教育〈学制期〉」「東京開成学校」 同上 * 寺崎昌男 「開成学校」 『[[日本史大事典]]』第2巻 [[平凡社]]、[[1993年]] * 同 「開成学校」 『[[日本歴史大事典]]』第1巻 [[小学館]]、[[2000年]] == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat}} *[[明六社]] *[[外国語学校 (明治初期)]] - [[東京外国語学校 (旧制)]] *[[畠山義成]]：東京開成学校初代校長 == 外部リンク == * [{{NDLDC|992865}} 『東京開成学校一覧　明治8年2月』] 東京開成学校編、1875年 * [http://hamadayori.com/hass-col/school/toudai.htm 発祥の地コレクション「東京大学発祥の地」] {{東京大学の源流・前身諸機関}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かいせいかつこう}} [[Category:日本の旧制高等教育機関]] [[Category:東京都の旧制教育機関]] [[Category:明治時代の教育]] [[Category:東京大学の歴史]] [[Category:開成所]] [[Category:19世紀日本の教育者|学校]] [[Category:日本の英語学校|歴史]] [[Category:日本のフランス語学校|歴史]] [[Category:日本のドイツ語学校|歴史]] [[Category:学校記事]]

'''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of Education|ja|文部省}}, located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}} but closed after the {{ill|Fall of Edo|ja|江戸開城}} in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the {{ill|Meirokusha|ja|明六社}} in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the {{ill|Bansho Shirabesho|ja|蕃書調所}}, established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary {{ill|Guido Verbeck|ja|グイド・フルベッキ}}, who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the {{ill|University|ja|大学校}}, later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)}}, a precursor to the {{ill|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|ja|東京外国語大学}}.
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)}} * {{ill|Hozumi Nobushige|ja|穂積陳重}} * {{ill|Hatoyama Kazuo|ja|鳩山和夫}} * {{ill|Miyake Setsurei|ja|三宅雪嶺}} * {{ill|Jigorō Kanō|ja|嘉納治五郎}} * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六}} * {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|ja|夏目漱石}} (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * {{ill|Guido Verbeck|ja|グイド・フルベッキ}} * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋}}, later moving to the {{ill|Hongo Campus|ja|東京大学本郷地区キャンパス}} by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Meirokusha|ja|明六社}} * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)}}

== Faculty == * {{ill|Guido Verbeck|ja|グイド・フルベッキ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, later moving to the {{ill|Hongo Campus|ja|東京大学本郷地区キャンパス|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Meirokusha|ja|明六社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

[[sn:Kaisei School]] [[ja:開成学校]] [[ko:가이세이 학교]] [[en:Kaisei School]]
<!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School-->

'''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of Education|ja|文部省|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft}} but closed after the {{ill|Fall of Edo|ja|江戸開城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the {{ill|Meirokusha|ja|明六社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the {{ill|Bansho Shirabesho|ja|蕃書調所|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary {{ill|Guido Verbeck|ja|グイド・フルベッキ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the {{ill|University|ja|大学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a precursor to the {{ill|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|ja|東京外国語大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hozumi Nobushige|ja|穂積陳重|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Hatoyama Kazuo|ja|鳩山和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Miyake Setsurei|ja|三宅雪嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Jigorō Kanō|ja|嘉納治五郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|ja|夏目漱石|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * {{ill|Guido Verbeck|ja|グイド・フルベッキ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, later moving to the {{ill|Hongo Campus|ja|東京大学本郷地区キャンパス|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Meirokusha|ja|明六社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

'''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Ministry of Education|ja|文部省|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Education|qq=}}, located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|qq=}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}} but closed after the {{ill|Fall of Edo|ja|江戸開城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fall of Edo|qq=}} in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the {{ill|Meirokusha|ja|明六社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meirokusha|qq=}} in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the {{ill|Bansho Shirabesho|ja|蕃書調所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bansho Shirabesho|qq=}}, established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}} in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary {{ill|Guido Verbeck|ja|グイド・フルベッキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guido Verbeck|qq=}}, who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the {{ill|University|ja|大学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University|qq=}}, later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=}}, a precursor to the {{ill|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|ja|東京外国語大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|qq=}}.
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=}} * {{ill|Hozumi Nobushige|ja|穂積陳重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi Nobushige|qq=}} * {{ill|Hatoyama Kazuo|ja|鳩山和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatoyama Kazuo|qq=}} * {{ill|Miyake Setsurei|ja|三宅雪嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyake Setsurei|qq=}} * {{ill|Jigorō Kanō|ja|嘉納治五郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigorō Kanō|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=}} * {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|ja|夏目漱石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Natsume Sōseki|qq=}} (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * {{ill|Guido Verbeck|ja|グイド・フルベッキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guido Verbeck|qq=}} * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=}}, later moving to the {{ill|Hongo Campus|ja|東京大学本郷地区キャンパス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo Campus|qq=}} by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Meirokusha|ja|明六社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meirokusha|qq=}} * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=}}
{{Tokyo University Predecessors}}

The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=}}
{{Tokyo University Predecessors}}

[[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.

=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.

In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=}}

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:明治時代の教育
revision.text:
'''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|qq=}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=}}

== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.

<!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--> [[Category:19th-century Japanese educators]] [[Category:日本のドイツ語学校]] [[Category:French international schools in Japan]] [[Category:日本の旧制高等教育機関]] [[Category:English conversation schools in Japan]] [[Category:明治時代の教育]] [[Category:東京大学の歴史]] [[Category:東京都の旧制教育機関]]

'''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|qq=}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=}}

<!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--> [[Category:19th-century Japanese educators]] [[Category:日本のドイツ語学校]] [[Category:French international schools in Japan]] [[Category:Higher education institutions in the Empire of Japan]] [[Category:English conversation schools in Japan]] [[Category:明治時代の教育]] [[Category:東京大学の歴史]] [[Category:東京都の旧制教育機関]] [[Category:開成所]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Tokyo Kaisei School}}

revision.comment: Bot: remove invalid enwiki interwiki links
revision.text:
'''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|qq=}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=}}
{{Tokyo University Predecessors}}

[[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=}}

The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=}}

The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.

The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=}}
