revision.comment: Merge [[Category:明治時代の教育]] into [[Category:Education of the Meiji Period]] via bot
revision.text:
'''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|qq=}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎}} (John Manjirō)

== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=}}

The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|qq=|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.

=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in 1870.

A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.

<!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--> [[Category:19th-century Japanese educators]] [[Category:German Language Schools in Japan]] [[Category:French international schools in Japan]] [[Category:Higher education institutions in the Empire of Japan]] [[Category:English conversation schools in Japan]] [[Category:Education of the Meiji Period]] [[Category:History of the University of Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Educational Institutions of Tokyo]] [[Category:Kaiseijo]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Tokyo Kaisei School}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|qq=|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=|1=Gongin Students|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634660}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=|1=Takeo Kikuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619799}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=|1=Yamaguchi Hanroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466694}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=|1=Gottfried Wagener|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q100645}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎|1=Nakahama Manjirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193167}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋|1=Hitotsubashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3347238}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|qq=|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}

In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=|1=Gongin Students|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634660}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|qq=|1=Takeo Kikuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619799}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|qq=|1=Yamaguchi Hanroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466694}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|qq=|1=Gottfried Wagener|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q100645}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎|1=Nakahama Manjirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193167}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋|1=Hitotsubashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3347238}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048

<!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--> [[Category:19th-century Japanese educators]] [[Category:German Language Schools in Japan]] [[Category:French international schools in Japan]] [[Category:Higher education institutions in the Empire of Japan]] [[Category:English conversation schools in Japan]] [[Category:Education of the Meiji Period]] [[Category:History of the University of Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Educational Institutions of Tokyo]] [[Category:Kaiseijo]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Tokyo Kaisei School}} {{wikidata link|Q10855049}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Kaisei School]][[ja:開成学校]][[ko:가이세이 학교]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|qq=|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|qq=|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in 1870.

It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|qq=|1=Gongin Students|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634660}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|qq=|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|qq=|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.

<!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--> [[Category:19th-century Japanese educators]] [[Category:German Language Schools in Japan]] [[Category:French international schools in Japan]] [[Category:Higher education institutions in the Empire of Japan]] [[Category:English conversation schools in Japan]] [[Category:Education of the Meiji Period]] [[Category:History of the University of Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Educational Institutions of Tokyo]] [[Category:Kaiseijo]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Tokyo Kaisei School}} {{wikidata link|Q10855049}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Kaisei School]][[ja:開成学校]][[ko:가이세이 학교]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|1=Gongin Students|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634660}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|1=Takeo Kikuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619799}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|1=Yamaguchi Hanroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466694}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|1=Gottfried Wagener|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q100645}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎|1=Nakahama Manjirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193167}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋|1=Hitotsubashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3347238}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}

In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|1=Takeo Kikuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619799}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|1=Yamaguchi Hanroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466694}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|1=Gottfried Wagener|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q100645}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎|1=Nakahama Manjirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193167}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋|1=Hitotsubashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3347238}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Kaisei School]][[ja:開成学校]][[ko:가이세이 학교]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|1=Gongin Students|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634660}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|1=Takeo Kikuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619799}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|1=Yamaguchi Hanroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466694}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|1=Gottfried Wagener|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q100645}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎|1=Nakahama Manjirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193167}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋|1=Hitotsubashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3347238}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}

It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|1=Gongin Students|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634660}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|1=Takeo Kikuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619799}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|1=Yamaguchi Hanroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466694}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|1=Gottfried Wagener|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q100645}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎|1=Nakahama Manjirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193167}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋|1=Hitotsubashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3347238}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}

== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|1=Gottfried Wagener|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q100645}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎|1=Nakahama Manjirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193167}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋|1=Hitotsubashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3347238}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048

<!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--><!--Kaisei School--> [[Category:19th-century Japanese educators]] [[Category:German Language Schools in Japan]] [[Category:French international schools in Japan]] [[Category:Higher education institutions in the Empire of Japan]] [[Category:English conversation schools in Japan]] [[Category:Education of the Meiji Period]] [[Category:History of the University of Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Educational Institutions of Tokyo]] [[Category:Kaiseijo]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Tokyo Kaisei School}} {{wikidata link|Q10855049}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tokyo Kaisei School]][[ja:開成学校]][[ko:가이세이 학교]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855049]] Wikidata name: Kaisei School [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kaisei School''' (開成学校) was an early Meiji-era educational institution under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], located in Tokyo Prefecture. It was one of the "specialized schools" of the time, representing higher education distinct from later vocational schools under the {{ill|Senmon Gakkō Rei|ja|専門学校令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmon Gakkō Rei|1=Senmon Gakkō Rei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3258590}}. [[File:Kaisei School1.jpg|thumb|right|270px|The later Kaisei School (1873)]]
== Overview == Kaisei School, as a government institution in early Meiji Japan, can be divided into two main periods: the "Early Kaisei School" (1868–1870) and the "Later Kaisei School" (1872–1877).
The early phase began with the revival of the {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}}, a school for foreign studies established in 1863 by the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but closed after the [[:en:Fall of Edo|Fall of Edo]] in 1868. The new Meiji government reopened it in September 1868 as Kaisei School.
The later phase began in 1872 when the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School. Renamed Kaisei School in 1873, it later became Tokyo Kaisei School and was integrated with {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}} in 1877 to form the {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}.
Kaisei School also influenced the establishment of the [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] in 1873, where many of its faculty and students were active members.
== History ==
=== Origins: Tokugawa Kaiseijo === {{Main|Kaiseijo}} Kaisei School traces its roots to the [[:en:Bansho Shirabesho|Bansho Shirabesho]], established in 1857 by the Tokugawa shogunate to study and teach Western knowledge. Renamed {{ill|Kaiseijo|ja|開成所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseijo|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655236}} in 1863, it became the direct predecessor of Kaisei School.
=== Early Kaisei School === {{wikisource|Newspaper Printing Ordinance}} The Kaiseijo was temporarily closed during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 but reopened by the Meiji government on September 12, 1868 (Lunisolar September12日). Officially renamed Kaisei School on January 17, 1869 (明治2nd year 1月17日), it resumed as a center for Western learning and translation under Dutch missionary [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]], who served as vice-principal.
Courses were offered in English, German, and French, reflecting the languages of the foreign instructors. In July 1869, the school became part of the [[:en:Daigakkō|University]], later renamed the {{ill|University South Campus|ja|大学南校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University South Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in 1870.
=== University South Campus === {{Main|University South Campus}} After internal conflicts caused the closure of the {{ill|University Main Campus|ja|大学本校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/University Main Campus|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大学校 (1869年)|1=Daigakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434269}} in August 1870, the South Campus effectively operated independently. It emphasized educating {{ill|Gongin Students|ja|貢進生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongin Students|1=Gongin Students|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634660}}, elite students chosen from across Japan to study English, French, or German.
In 1871, the campus was renamed "Nanko" and briefly closed for reorganization. It reopened later that year, focusing on general foreign language education.
=== Later Kaisei School (Tokyo Kaisei School) === In 1872, Nanko was reorganized as the First University District First Middle School under the new {{ill|Education System Order|ja|学制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Education System Order|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Education System Order|WD=Q11448123}}. By 1873, it transitioned into Kaisei School, with English as the primary language of instruction. The curriculum expanded to include five specialized courses: law, chemistry, engineering, mining, and arts, though French and German continued in limited capacities.
In November 1873, the foreign language department separated to form the {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, a precursor to the [[:en:Tokyo University of Foreign Studies|Tokyo University of Foreign Studies]].
Renamed Tokyo Kaisei School in 1874, it added a preparatory department and courses for practical engineering training. In 1875, the first group of government-sponsored overseas students, selected from its Ranks, began their studies abroad.
By 1877, Tokyo Kaisei School merged with Tokyo Medical School to form {{ill|(Old) Tokyo University|ja|東京大学 (1877-1886)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/(Old) Tokyo University|1=Tokyo University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524659}}, contributing its law, science, and literature departments.
== Notable Alumni == * {{ill|Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|ja|菊池武夫 (法律家)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuchi Takeo (lawyer)|1=Takeo Kikuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619799}} * [[:en:Hozumi Nobushige|Hozumi Nobushige]] * [[:en:Kazuo Hatoyama|Hatoyama Kazuo]] * [[:en:Miyake Setsurei|Miyake Setsurei]] * [[:en:Kanō Jigorō|Jigorō Kanō]] * {{ill|Yamaguchi Hanroku|ja|山口半六|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Hanroku|1=Yamaguchi Hanroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466694}} * [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]] (elder brother Natsume Daisuke)
== Faculty == * [[:en:Guido Verbeck|Guido Verbeck]] * {{ill|Gottfried Wagener|ja|ゴットフリード・ワグネル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gottfried Wagener|1=Gottfried Wagener|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q100645}} * {{ill|Nakayama Manjirō|ja|中浜万次郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Manjirō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ジョン万次郎|1=Nakahama Manjirō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193167}} (John Manjirō)
== Campus and Legacy == The school operated at its original location in {{ill|Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja|神田一ツ橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Hitotsubashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一ツ橋|1=Hitotsubashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3347238}}, later moving to the [[:en:Hongō campus|Hongo Campus]] by 1885. A monument commemorating "The Birthplace of the University of Tokyo" stands at the original site.
== Related Literature == ; Books * Masao Terasaki, ''The History of the University of Tokyo: Pioneer of the University System,'' Kodansha Academic Library, 2007 ISBN 9784061597990 * Ikuo Amano, ''The Birth of Universities (Part I): The Era of Imperial Universities,'' Chuko Shinsho, 2009 ISBN 9784121020048
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Meirokusha|Meirokusha]] * {{ill|Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Foreign Language School (Meiji)|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}
